rulings.Case Title: CHARLES E. YEAGER et al VS. WILD, CARTER & TIPTON et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For A Protective Order Re: The Deposition Of Charles J Murray And For Imposition Of A Monetary Sanction In The Amount Of $1,060 Against Pltf Victoria Yeagerf
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, January 29, 2013, at 9:00 am, line 1, DEFENDANT DE LA PENA & MCDONALD, LLP, MCDONALD, STEVEN E. (SUED AS STEPHEN E. MCDONALD) Notice Of Motion For A Protective Order Re: The Deposition Of Charles J Murray And For Imposition Of A Monetary Sanction In The Amount Of $1,060 Against Plaintiff Victoria Yeager Pro Tem Judge Robert Kane, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The parties are to appear. With regard to the three pending motions, the parties are directed to meet and confer as to specific times, dates and places for the depositions at issue that will be reduced to an order that will not be subject to change absent good cause. If the parties cannot reach an agreement, they should be prepared to explain their position to the court. In addition, if sanctions are still being sought, the parties should be prepared to argue whether sanctions should be awarded and if so in what amount. =302/JPT

rulings.Case Title: ELVIS BOKAN BY AND THROUGH HIS GUARDIAN AD LITEM, et al VS. THE BLUE SHIELD OF CALIFORNIA LIFE & HEALTH et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, January 29, 2013, Line 3, DEFENDANT THE BLUE SHIELD OF CALIFORNIA LIFE & HEALTH's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. The motion for summary judgment is denied. Defendant failed to maintain its initial summary judgment burden. Defendant does not sufficiently address whether it breached the contract and acted in bad faith by failing to make timely payments during the 31-day period after Elvis Bokan's birth. The Court notes that the complaint does not clearly raise this theory of liability but the Court liberally construes the complaint under CCP 452. In addition, there is no prejudice to defendant. Defendant knew that this was a basis for liability. Footnote 5 of defendant's moving points and authorities refers to the issue, but the separate statement of undisputed material facts does not show that the claim cannot be established. The Court cannot summarily adjudicate whether defendant breached the contract and acted in bad faith by failing to make the March payments. The Court cannot split a cause of action. Defendant's citation to Lilienthal & Fowler v. Sup. Crt. (1993) 12 Cal.App.4th 1848, 1854-55, is distinguishable, where the acts alleged were "separate and distinct wrongful act[s]." Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure 437c(g) and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/MJM)

rulings.Case Title: DIANE LINEHAN VS. BAXTER INTERNATIONAL, INC. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn To Quash In The Alt Issue A Protective Order Regarding The Ntc Of Taking A Videotaped Deposition And Production Of Documents Of Helena Lim
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, January 29, 2013, Line 5, DEFENDANT CATHOLIC HEALTHCARE WEST DBA ST MARY'S MEDICAL'S MOTION To Quash OR In The AltERNATIVE Issue A Protective Order Regarding The NOTICE Of Taking A Videotaped Deposition And Production Of Documents Of Helena Lim. Motion to quash is denied and alternate request for protective order is granted in part. It is undisputed that the prospective deponent is seriously ill. However, deponent is potentially in possession of information essential to the trial of this case about the amount of Dilaudid administered to Plaintiff which is reasonably calculated to lead to admissible evidence regarding Plaintiff's cause of death. Deponent was the director of pharmacy at the time the pharmacy was asked for the re-creation of Plaintiff's medical records and was the only St. Mary's employee to provide information to the S.F. Medical Examine about the timing, dosage, and concentration of Dilaudid. Deponent's deposition was first sought on May 9, 2012. Deponent was able to provide a detailed declaration in opposition to this motion. The declaration suggests that the deposition will not be lengthy, given her limited involvement and her lack of "specific memory" as to certain events. Under all of the circumstances, Plaintiff shall be permitted to take the deposition, ask questions and cross-examine her on the declaration. The parties shall meet and confer on taking the deposition at a place and time that is the least burdensome on the Deponent, including at her home if Deponent so requests, with as many breaks as necessary. The total time of deposition questioning shall not last for more than one hour. Sanctions are denied. The court will not provide a court reporter for this discovery hearing. If any party wishes a reporter, the parties must meet and confer and agree on a single reporter. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/MJM)

rulings.Case Title: HYATT CORPORATION A DELAWARE CORPORATION VS. UNITE HERE! LOCAL2 A LABOR ORGANIZATION et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Form Interrogatory 12.1 Special Interrogatory 4 And Request For Production Of Documents 15
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, January 29, 2013, Line 6, DEFENDANT UNITE HERE! LOCAL2 A LABOR ORGANIZATION'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Form Interrogatory 12.1 Special Interrogatory 4 And Request For Production Of Documents 15. Granted. In response to Form Interrogatory 12.1 and Special Interrogatory 4, Plaintiff's response includes "various unidentified guests or patrons of Plaintiff." The disclosure of names and addressess of potential witnesses is a routine and essential part of pretrial discovery. Puerto v. Superior Court, 158 Cal. App. 4th 1242, 1250 (2008). In fact, Plaintiff has already agreed to such disclosures with regard to certain hotel patrons. There is no basis to distinguish the disclosure of these witnesses from other similarly situated potential hotel guest witnesses, or to limit Plaintiff's disclosure to those hotel patron witnesses it has already agreed to identify. Balancing the privacy interests of the guests with the interests of the requesting party, fairness to the litigants, and the consequences of granting access to the information, the Court grants the motion, subject to certain conditions to protect the privacy of the information sought. Within 20 days, Plaintiff must provide supplemental responses by providing the names and contact information of all guests who were present during the relevant dates. These responses are subject to the following limitations: (1) this information shall be subject to a protective order used only by the attorneys and their agents for use in this case only, and the information shall not be shared with or disseminated to the defendant union, its members or employees, except as may be divulged in a judicial proceeding. Defendant has proposed the additional protective measure of an opt-out privacy notice to be sent to hotel patrons. (Defendant's MPA at 8, l. 7-11.) The parties are to meet and confer on this procedure. If Plaintiff agrees to this additional procedural step, the Court will make the opt-out procedure a part of its order. If Plaintiff does not wish to add this procedural step, the Order will be granted as set forth above. The court will not provide a court reporter for this discovery hearing. If any party wishes a reporter, the parties must meet and confer and agree on a single reporter. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/MJM)

rulings.Case Title: KASOWITZ, BENSON, TORRES & FRIEDMAN, LLP A NEW VS. JOAN MACK et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Production With Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, January 29, 2013, at 9:00 am, line 2, Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Production With Request For Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Robert Kane, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The parties appear to agree that the motion was not filed within the 45-day statutory time limit. Based on the foregoing, the motion is denied as there does not appear to be authority for relief under CCP §473. (Sexton v. Superior Court (1997) 58 Cal.App.4th 1403, 1410 [quasi-jurisdictional requirement requiring denial].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to rkane1089@aol.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/JPT

rulings.Case Title: KASOWITZ, BENSON, TORRES & FRIEDMAN, LLP A NEW VS. JOAN MACK et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, January 29, 2013, Line 19, CROSS DEFENDANT KASOWITZ, BENSON, TORRES & FRIEDMAN, LLP'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT. Judge Miller, who will be hearing this demurrer, discloses that she conducted a settlement conference on August 9, 2010 in Brooks v. Topel, et al. (Case CGC-09-486251), an unrelated case involving defendant Kasowitz, Benson, that is mentioned in Cross-Complainant's opposition to the demurrer and in Cross-Complainant's Request for Judicial Notice. This fact does not affect Judge Miller's ability to be fair and impartial in hearing the demurrer and motion to strike in this case. Tentative ruling: Sustained with leave to amend on standing grounds and overruled on all other grounds. Regarding standing, Cross-Complainant is given leave to amend to allege facts in the body of the complaint that Cross-Complainant has standing to prosecute claims on behalf of third-party Darren Mack. Regarding the actual innocence rule, that rule does not apply to this case because the primary right sought to be vindicated by Cross-Complainant in each cause of action is not the right to competent representation. Regarding the statute of limitations, it does not appear from the face of the First Amended Cross-Complaint that any cause of action is barred by the statute of limitations. Cross-Complainant has alleged facts sufficient to show that the discovery rule may apply. Regarding the litigation privilege, it does not appear from the face of the First Amended Cross-Complaint that any of the claims are affirmatively barred. Regarding notice under the CLRA, even assuming notice was not properly provided, failure to provide notice does not preclude the filing of a claim under the CLRA solely for injunctive relief as is pled here. Civil Code 1782(d). Other than as discussed above, the First Amended Cross-Complaint and all causes of action contained therein are properly pled. The objection to Cross-Complainant's supplemental opposition is sustained; the papers are late filed without leave of Court. Cross-Defendant's Request for Judicial Notice is granted. The Court denies Cross-Complainants' Request for Judicial Notice of Exhibits 1 and 2 ("existence, content and truth" of the Third Amended Complaint and an order "Granting and Denying Defendants' Motion for Summary Adjudication; Order Re Motion to Dismiss," both filed in an unrelated Case CGC-09-486251. They are irrelevant to the legal question before the Court on this demurrer. The Court grants Cross-Complainants' Request for Judicial Notice of Exhibit 3, which is unopposed. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/MJM)

rulings.Case Title: JAMES J FRANICEVICH et al VS. ROBERT HOWARD PETERSON
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, January 29, 2013, Line 17, DEFENDANT ROBERT PETERSON'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. Sustained, without leave to amend, as to the first, second, and third. Sustained with leave to amend as to the fourth cause of action. For the first and second causes of action, Plaintiffs are not intended beneficiaries as they are not the subject of a specific bequest by Defendant attorney's now deceased client. Rather Plaintiffs are potential beneficiaries, and Defendant owed no duty to Plaintiffs as potential beneficiaries of his client's estate. This is because "a testator's lawyer [does not] owe a duty of care to a nonclient who alleges [he] was a potential beneficiary of the testator's estate in the absence of an executed will or trust instrument expressly reflecting the testator's intent." (Chang v. Lederman (2009) 172 Cal.App.4th 67, 86.) The claims for legal malpractice and contract fail as a matter of law. The third cause of action "tortious interference with prospective economic advantage," sounds in negligence, and Plaintiffs cannot prevail because Defendant owed them no duty, as set forth above. As to the fourth cause of action, entitled "intentional interference with prospective economic advantage," the demurrer is sustained. This cause of action requires knowing interference and intentional conduct that was wrongful by some measure other than the interference, which is not alleged. However, Plaintiffs appear in their moving papers to be seeking to allege intentional interference with expectation of inheritance. See Beckwith v. Dahl (2012) 205 Cal.App.4th 1039, 1057. Plaintiffs are given leave to amend to plead, if they can do so in good faith, a cause of action against this Defendant and material facts to support that cause of action. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/MJM)
